auto_merge.commit_message:
Fixes https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/7767

There were a few issues:
- Cython assumed it could call `__annotate__` with no arguments, which is not true
  for the functions CPython generates
- Calling it eagerly is the wrong thing to do anyway: it loses lazy evaluation
  of annotations. This PR adds lazy evaluation similar to what   CPython does.
auto_merge.commit_title: Fix cyfunction annotate setter on Python 3.14 (GH-7768)

repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body:
Fixes #7767.

There were a few issues:
- Cython assumed it could call `__annotate__` with no arguments, which is not true for the functions CPython generates
- Calling it eagerly is the wrong thing to do anyway: it loses lazy evaluation of annotations. This PR adds lazy evaluation similar to what CPython does.

comment_data.body: Should this be `PyDict_GetItemStringRef` on versions that support it?

I think the most important test is that `wraps` works with a non-cyfunction and a cyfunction (presumably in both directions, although I think only the cyfunction wrapping a non-cyfunction direction was broken).

In that that's the behaviour that this is trying to support and everything else is really implementation details.

One consequence of this change is that looking up `__annotations__` on a cyfunction without any annotations will now always instantiate `__dict__` as a way of checking  (which most cyfunctions don't have) so it ends up being relatively expensive

Personally I think it'd be clearer to move most of this in `__Pyx_CyFunction_get_annotations_locked` rather than jumping in an out of critical sections. I'm aware that the critical section might end up interrupted but it's less to think about, and I don't think critical sections on cyfunctions are heavily contested (they're only really used for the introspection-type attributes)

This code is changing a bit but I think the separate critical sections are cleaner, since various parts of this code may call arbitrary Python code (e.g., calling `__annotate__`), and there might be subtle deadlocks if we do that while holding the critical section.
Yeah, good point. Changes I'm about to push will avoid materializing the dict when we don't need to.
Added some tests.
Made it call this on recent versions.
PyPy seems to provide `_PyObject_GetDictPtr()`, not sure about GraalPython but probably not. It would be better to use `PyObject_GenericGetDict()` outside of `CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON`.

```suggestion
#if __PYX_LIMITED_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030d0000
```

I think we can afford to instantiate the string constant upfront. That avoids the need to call the `…String()` C-API variants.
```suggestion
    return PyDict_SetItem(dict, PYIDENT("__annotate__"), value);
```
etc. in all other places.

Could you please reverse this condition and swap the implementation blocks? I prefer moving special cases first and the general/normal case last, both because it makes special cases easier to remove later (due to dependency EOL) and because they often end up accompanied with more special cases, and then they chain more cleanly if put first. The same applies to various other places below.
It looks like this function could benefit from an error return code to distinguish between "don't have a dict" and "failure while trying to get the dict".

This is really just
```suggestion
    result = __Pyx_XNewRef(op->func_annotations);
```
This appears in the wrong spot (in an already released version section). You can merge master and move it up to the future version, or I can do it later.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -255,6 +255,9 @@ Bugs fixed
   ``@functools.wraps`` work in Python 3.14+.
   (Github issue :issue:`7675`)
 
+* Assigning a Python 3.14+ ``.__annotate__`` function to a Cython compiled function no
+  longer evaluates annotations eagerly.
+

comment_data.body: Can we be sure that `op->func_annotations` holds the correct value if it's set already, or should we overwrite it?

> PyPy seems to provide `_PyObject_GetDictPtr()`, not sure about GraalPython but probably not. It would be better to use `PyObject_GenericGetDict()` outside of `CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON`.

It looks like we use `_PyObject_GetDictPtr()` elsewhere so I think it's fine.

I think you could argue that it's an arbitrary timing thing that some race has happened, both values were the right answer at some point, and it doesn't really which you return and which you persist. I don't think there's a single logical order that makes sense.

If I'd have written this, I'd have probably returned the existing value. But I don't have a reason to claim that this is a more correct choice.

I don't feel particularly strongly about this so happy to leave it as is since we have something that works currently.

I think critical sections should be deadlock-free. So the arbitrary Python code in `__annotate__` could definitely end up using the same critical section and breaking it, but should never deadlock. You couldn't assume that `func_annotate` was unchanged after the call to the arbitrary Python code though.

Thinking about this more: overwriting the existing value might be inconsistent (because you wouldn't have called `__annotate__` had there been an existing value). I think the choice of what to return really is arbitrary.

So I'd claim this implements (one of the) sensible options and so is fine.
Right, I double checked and it's a historically named, de facto public function. Both PyPy and GraalPy provide it. Fine then.
Actually CPython unconditionally overwrites func_annotations in this case, so I think it's more consistent to do that.
Apparently there's no way for it to fail while getting a dict. But I put the dict getting in a separate function for clarity.
`PyObject_GenericGetDict()` ?
Ah thanks, I was looking for a SETREF equivalent in Cython
Done

I think we only need one of these now, the rest is used in order.
```suggestion
static int __Pyx_CyFunction_set_annotate_in_dict_if_exists(PyObject *op_in, PyObject *value); /*proto*/
```

```suggestion
#if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_PYPY
    *dict = PyObject_GenericGetDict(op_in, NULL);
#elif CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API || PY_VERSION_HEX < 0x030C0000
```

It looks to me like PyPy is a bit of a mess with their various dict getting functions. They don't seem to use `__dictoffset__` and `_PyObject_GetDictPtr` doesn't seem to get the same thing as `PyObject_GenericGetDict`.

Suggestion above will work, but will do an unnecessary instantiation. I haven't managed to find anything better, so I suggest that we live with it for PyPy.

(I'll send PyPy a bug report on this)

```suggestion
```

commit.message: Fix cyfunction annotate setter on Python 3.14
Avoid materializing cyfunction dict for annotate lookup
Avoid creating dict when setting cyfunction annotations
Avoid calling cyfunction annotate in critical section
